name: Redis 7
license: RSALv2 / SSPLv1 for 7.4.x–7.8.x; BSDv3 for older 7.2.x and earlier; Redis 8 adds AGPLv3 option
what: In-memory data structure server used for cache, queues, ephemeral state, rate limiting, and some workflow backplanes.
solves: Low-latency caching, job queues, session support, pub/sub or streams, rate limits, and burst absorption between systems.
users: Engineering and platform teams that need fast volatile storage or queue primitives for web apps and automation systems.
fit: Strong technical fit because many candidate apps use Redis for queues or cache, but the licensing transition requires deliberate human review before expanding use.
runtime: Single process server with persistence options, replication/sentinel/cluster modes, and multiple data structures including lists, streams, and sorted sets.
components: Redis server, persistent volume if AOF/RDB enabled, optional sentinel/cluster peers, application clients, and observability hooks.
deploy: Single-node with persistence for smaller app clusters; HA or managed equivalent only when justified by queue criticality and recovery targets.
deps: RAM headroom, persistent storage if durability matters, monitoring, auth/TLS, and app-level retry logic.
datastores: Redis is the datastore; often paired with PostgreSQL or MongoDB.
auth: ACLs, passwords, TLS, network segmentation, and client-specific credentials.
apis: RESP protocol, libraries, pub/sub, streams, queues, and exporter metrics.
security: Network exposure is dangerous. Use TLS or private networking, ACLs, secret rotation, and memory/eviction monitoring.
setup_path: Use official Redis 7 packages or containers only after licensing is approved internally; enable persistence only where required and document eviction policy explicitly.
env: REDIS_PASSWORD, appendonly/save settings, maxmemory, eviction policy, TLS certs, and network binding.
persistence: Choose AOF/RDB deliberately. Do not pretend volatile cache and durable queue workloads have the same recovery requirements.
operations: Memory pressure review, eviction monitoring, persistence checks, replica health, and version/license review before upgrades.
failure_points: OOM kills, silent key eviction, unauthenticated network exposure, and using Redis as a durable queue without operational guardrails.
integration: Needed by Novu, n8n queue mode, and many web apps for caches or background work. It fits the stack, but licensing must be surfaced early to legal and procurement stakeholders.
verify: Confirm whether Hylono is comfortable with Redis 7 licensing, whether Valkey is a preferable future path, and which workloads genuinely require Redis rather than database-backed queues.
risk: Moderate platform risk because it can become a hidden dependency and the licensing change affects procurement and redistribution decisions.
gdpr: Usually stores transient operational data, but payloads may still contain personal data if queue design is sloppy. Minimize and expire sensitive values.
recommendation: KEEP FOR EXISTING USE; REQUIRE LICENSE REVIEW BEFORE EXPANSION
overlap: Possible future substitution candidate with Valkey for some workloads.
codex_read_first: Read the licensing page and the actual app dependencies before treating Redis as a default choice.
codex_best_path: Retain Redis for apps that explicitly need it, but avoid adding new business-critical designs that depend on Redis durability without a clear recovery plan.
codex_never_assume: Never assume all Redis 7 builds are permissively licensed. Never assume cache settings are safe for queue workloads.

assets.name: Official license page
assets.notes: Critical human-review source for current licensing.

sources.why: Used to identify current 7.x point release researched.
